text: Lecture	  38	  –	  Nociception	  and	  Pain	   	   Objectives	   • Review	  the	  peripheral	  pain	  receptors	  &	  associated	  afferent	  fibres-­‐nociceptors	   • Review	  central	  pain	  pathways	  (spinothalamic	  pathway)	   • Describe	  and	  explain	  visceral	  referred	  pain	   • Distinguish	  between	  nociception	  &	  pain	   • Describe	  variations	  and	  modulation	  of	  pain	  and	  basis	  of	  treatments	   	   Nociceptors	   • Have	  bare	  nerve	  ends	   • Respond	  to	  damaging	  forms	  of	  stimulus	  energy	  ie	  excessive	  force	   • Receptors:	   o Mechanical,	  thermal	  and	  chemical	   energy	  or	  multimodal	  (all)	   	   Fibre	  groups	  of	  nociceptors	  	   • These	  convey	  different	  aspects	  of	  pain	   o Group	  Aδ	  -­‐	  small	  diameter	  myelinated	   (fast	  sharp	  pain)	   o Group	  C	  -­‐	  small	  diameter	   Unmyelinated	  (slow	  pain)	   • Sensation	  of	  pain	  is	  firstly	  sharp,	  immediate	   followed	  by	  a	  dull	  and	  slow	  pain/ache	   	   Central	  Pain	  Pathways	  (Anterolateral/Spinothalamic)	   • Cross	  at	  spinal	  cord	  level	   • Projections	  to:	   o Primary	  signal	  to	  Somatosensory	  cortex	   o Cingulate	  and	  Insula	  (limbic	  system)	  provide	  emotional	  association	  to	  pain.	   o Reticular	  Formation	  which	  is	  part	  of	  hypothalamus	  that	  controls	  the	  arousal	  and	  autonomic	   response	  eg	  changes	  in	  HR	  and	  BP	  (the	  reticular	  formation	  is	  part	  of	  the	  brainstem	  is	  it	  not?)	   o Central	  Lateral	  nucleus	  provides	  a	  generalised	  response	  to	  pain	   • There	  are	  no	  specific	  regions	  in	  brain/Somatopic	  map	  for	  organs.	  Thus	  this	  visceral	  pain	  seems	  to	  come	   from	  skin	  and	  hence	  we	  get	  referred	  pain	   o Occurs	  as	  these	  organs	  synapse	  with	  the	  same	  interneurons	  as	  the	  skin	  (convergence)	   	   Nociception	  vs	  Pain	   • Nociception:	  The	  physical	  process	  of	  excitation	  of	  specific	  sensory	  neurons	  by	  potentially	  damaging	   stimuli	   • Pain:	  The	  subjective	  perception.	  Can	  occur	  in	  the	  absence	  of	  nociception	  (Neuropathic	  Pain).	   	   Neuropathic	  Pain	   • A	  prime	  example	  of	  this	  is	  phantom	  pain	   • Abnormal	  activity	  (APs)	  in	  the	  pain	  pathways	   • Caused	  by	   o lesion	  to	  that	  thalamic	  regions,	  can	  occur	  after	  strokes	   o peripheral	  neuropathies	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   3 Two fibre groups carry nociceptive information •Group A(small diameter myelinated) •Group C (small diameter unmyelinated) From Kandel et al Perceived pain Modulation	  of	  Pain	  sensitisation	   • Hyperalgesia	  –	  increased	  intensity	  of	  pain	  caused	  by	  stimuli	  that	  are	  normally	  painful	   • One	  mechanism:	   o Tissue	  damage	  causes	  inflammation	  due	  to	  release	  of	  mediators	  (eg	  K+,	  Histamine,	   Prostaglandins	  etc)	  that	  increase	  the	  excitability	  of	  these	  nerve	  endings.	   • Allodynia	  –	  pain	  caused	  by	  stimulus	  that	  is	  not	  normally	  painful.	   	   Intrinsic	  Analgesic	  mechanisms	   • Interactions	  between	  ascending	  nociceptive	  and	  other	   somatosensory	  inputs	   o C	  fibre	  (pain)	  synapses	  with	  second	  order	  neurons.	   o Second	  order	  neuron	  can	  also	  receive	  inhibitory	  signal	  from	   Aβ	  fibres	  (Mechanosensitive)	   o If	  there	  is	  mechanical	  stimulation	  of	  the	  painful	  area,	  it	  will	   inhibit/subdue	  the	  signal	  of	  the	  second	  order	  neuron	    Eg	  when	  we	  rub	  on	  a	  painful	  area	  the	  pain	  is	   reduced.	   o Thermoreceptors	  have	  similar	  effects	  to	  mechanoreceptors	   (decrease	  sensitivity	  to	  pain)	  	   • Descending	  pathways	  can	  control	  transmission	  of	  pain	  information-­‐ the	  “analgesic	  pathway”	   o 	  Higher	  order	  neurons	  of	  dorsal	  gray	  matter	  can	  inhibit	  the	   relaying	  of	  pain	  in	  second	  order	  neurons	   o Nt	  is	  enkephalin	  an	  endogenous	  opioid.	    Acts	  on	  opiod	  receptors	  of	  the	  presynaptic	  and	  post	  synaptic	  to	  inhibit	  pain	    Provides	  the	  basis	  of	  opiod	  drugs	  eg	  morphine	    There	  are	  attempts	  to	  activate	  inhibitory	  pathways	  by	  electrical	  means	  or	  stimulate	  the	   release	  of	  endogenous	  opioids	  (activate	  or	  mimic	  analgesic	  pathways)	   	   Interventions	  to	  control	  pain	   • Local	  anaesthetics	  block	  action	  potential	  conduction	  in	  nociceptive	  nerve	  fibres	   • Anti-­‐inflammatory	  drugs-­‐reduce	  hyperalgesia	  and	  allodynia	   • Mechanical	  stimulation,	  TENS	  (transcutaneous	  electrical	  stimulation),	  heat	  &	  other	  methods	  may	  activate	   pain	  suppressing	  pathways.	   • Opiates-­‐effective	  drugs	  for	  pain	  originating	  below	  thalamus	   • How	  to	  treat	  central	  pain	  of	  thalamic	  or	  cortical	  origin?	  Unknown	  as	  yet.	  	   8 Interactions between ascending nociceptive and other somatosensory inputs Mechanosensitive afferents activate inhibitory interneurons in spinal cord From Kandel et al Circuitry in dorsal gray matter Descending analgesic pathway 8 Descending analgesic pathway
